flashcards.back: A connected graph G is biconnected if it remains connected after removing any single vertex (and all edges incident to it). Formally, for any vertex v, G-v is still connected.

flashcards.front: What is an articulation point (cut vertex)?
flashcards.back: A vertex v in a connected graph G is an articulation point if removing v (and all edges incident on v) disconnects the graph, i.e., G-v has more than one connected component.
An edge e in a connected graph G is a bridge if removing e disconnects the graph. A bridge is a special case where the edge itself is critical for connectivity.
A biconnected component (or block) is a maximal biconnected subgraph of G. Any two biconnected components share at most one vertex, and this vertex is necessarily an articulation point.

flashcards.front: What is the discovery time (disc[u]) in DFS?
flashcards.back: disc[u] is the timestamp when vertex u is first visited during DFS. It represents the order of discovery in the depth-first search tree.

flashcards.front: What is the low value (low[u]) in biconnectivity algorithm?
flashcards.back: low[u] is the minimum discovery time reachable from u by taking zero or more tree edges followed by at most one back edge. Formally: low[u] = min(disc[u], min{disc[w] | (u,w) is a back edge}, min{low[child] | child is a DFS tree child of u})

flashcards.front: State the articulation point theorem condition.
flashcards.back: A vertex u (except root) is an articulation point iff there exists a child v of u in DFS tree such that low[v] >= disc[u]. The root is an articulation point iff it has two or more children in the DFS tree.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between bridges and biconnected components?
flashcards.back: Every bridge in a graph belongs to exactly one biconnected component. An edge is a bridge iff it is the only edge connecting its endpoints in the DFS tree and low[child] > disc[parent].

flashcards.front: Why does the root of DFS tree need special handling for articulation points?
flashcards.back: The root has no ancestors, so low value comparison doesn't apply. Root is an articulation point iff it has two or more DFS tree children (meaning removing it separates at least two subtrees).

flashcards.front: How are biconnected components extracted using a stack?
flashcards.back: During DFS, push each edge onto a stack when traversed. When low[v] >= disc[u] for a child v, pop edges from stack until (u,v) is popped - these edges form one biconnected component.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of Tarjan's algorithm for biconnected components?
flashcards.back: O(V + E) - Same as DFS. We visit each vertex once and each edge twice (undirected graph), with constant-time stack operations.

flashcards.front: Can two biconnected components share more than one vertex?
flashcards.back: No. Two distinct biconnected components can intersect in at most one vertex, and this vertex must be an articulation point. This is the vertex connectivity property.
